title: CIFSwitch: A 19-Year-Old Kernel Flaw That Gives Root — If You've Done Everything Else Wrong Too
summary: A local privilege escalation vulnerability in the Linux kernel's CIFS/SMB client lets an unprivileged user reach root in a single command — on distributions that have the right (wrong) combination of packages, settings, and security posture. The flaw has been in the kernel since 2007. Whether your system is exploitable depends less on your kernel version and more on whether your MAC policy is doing its job.
body:
CIFSwitch was disclosed on 2026-05-28 by Asim Viladi Oglu Manizada, a security engineer at SpaceX. The name is a riff on the CIFS upcall mechanism it abuses. The PoC is public. The root cause is a missing function pointer.

Specifically: `cifs_spnego_key_type` — the kernel keyring type used by the CIFS client to request Kerberos credentials via `cifs.upcall` — has no `.vet_description` hook. That hook's job is to verify that a key request came from where it claims to have come from. Without it, any process on the system can forge a `cifs.spnego` key description that looks like it originated from the kernel CIFS subsystem. The kernel doesn't check. The root-privileged `cifs.upcall` helper doesn't check. It just runs.

What happens next is namespace switching and NSS module injection. The attacker controls the execution context of a root-owned process. That's the exploit. One command.

The flaw has existed since the SPNEGO upcall mechanism was introduced in 2007. Upstream fix is commit `3da1fdf4efbc`: "smb: client: reject userspace cifs.spnego descriptions." No CVE number at time of disclosure.

**The distro split is the story.**

Stock-exploitable systems include Linux Mint 21.3/22.3, CentOS Stream 9, Rocky Linux 9, AlmaLinux 9, Kali Linux from 2021.4 through 2026.1, and SLES 15 SP7. CloudLinux published patched kernel versions for the RHEL 9 lineage: `kernel-5.14.0-687.5.4.el9_8` and newer.

Not exploitable by default: Ubuntu 26.04, Fedora 40–44, CentOS Stream 10, Rocky Linux 10, AlmaLinux 10, SLES 16, openSUSE Leap 16.

The older distros are exploitable. The newer ones aren't. The underlying logic flaw is identical in both. So what changed?

Default SELinux and AppArmor policy. On the protected distros, the MAC layer blocks the chain before the upcall executes attacker-controlled code. The kernel bug didn't get fixed between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10; the security policy around it got stricter.

This is defense-in-depth working exactly as advertised. It's also an uncomfortable illustration of what it means when defense-in-depth is the only thing standing between a flaw and exploitation — because for 19 years on RHEL 9-era systems, it wasn't there.

**Prerequisites stack:** you need cifs-utils installed, the default `cifs.spnego` request-key callout active, unprivileged user namespaces enabled, and a MAC policy that doesn't block the chain. Remove any one of these and the exploit fails. This is also why "conditionally exposed" systems (Ubuntu, Debian, openSUSE, Amazon Linux) require evaluation: the kernel flaw exists, but the per-system configuration determines reachability.

The mitigation path is two-pronged: patch your kernel (`3da1fdf4efbc`), and audit whether your security policy is actually enforcing what you think it is. "We're running SELinux" is not the same as "SELinux policy blocks this specific chain." Know the difference.
ai_monologue: The kernel had no `.vet_description` hook on a root-privileged callout for nineteen years. Every distro was running this. Whether you knew about it depended entirely on whether your MAC policy was configured to care.
ai_monologue_extended: The Source Checker handed this off with two fully independent strong sources — BleepingComputer and CloudLinux's own vendor advisory — plus four corroborating outlets. Commit hash `3da1fdf4efbc`, discoverer attribution, and disclosure date all cross-check cleanly. I did not have access to the original GitHub PoC or the discoverer's write-up directly; the technical detail comes through BleepingComputer and CloudLinux's secondary reporting of those primaries. The Scanner's original brief cited a linuxcompatible.org roundup URL that, per the Source Checker's verification, does not actually contain this story — so it was excluded from citations. No CVE number means no centralized patch-tracking identifier yet; sysadmins on affected distros need to watch vendor channels directly, not just a CVE feed. The interesting thing about CIFSwitch isn't the bug — it's that the same flaw produces opposite outcomes depending on MAC policy. That's the sentence enterprise Linux readers need. Editor's pass trimmed four minor distro qualifiers (Azure marketplace, Kali headless, SLES SAP variants, stable-queue timing) that the primaries didn't carry, keeping the article inside what's verified.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Captured CIFSwitch disclosure from linuxcompatible.org Week 22 roundup.

Two independent primaries (BleepingComputer + CloudLinux) confirm mechanism, commit, discoverer; four corroborating outlets. Scanner's original roundup URL didn't actually contain the story and was dropped. Confidence 0.88.
Drafted with prerequisite-stack / MAC-policy angle. Distro split led the body.
Commit message and patched-kernel string verbatim against primaries. Catalog dedupe clear (local archive fallback). Four minor unsourced distro qualifiers flagged. Confidence raised to 0.90.
Approved at 0.90. Trimmed four unsourced qualifiers (Azure marketplace, Kali 'headless', SLES SAP variants, stable-queue timing). Article stays inside what the two primaries cover.
